input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **MABI MAX 100 industrial robot with six axes**, specifically focusing on its **first axis** as the primary physical twin system. This system operates within the **manufacturing automation domain**, where precision motion control and friction management are critical. The physical twin is characterized by **real-time kinematic and dynamic behavior**, including **joint-level motion accuracy** and **friction-induced deviations**, which are captured via **sensorized feedback** (e.g., encoders, torque sensors) for digital shadowing and twinning purposes. The system’s operational domain requires **high-resolution data acquisition** to enable predictive maintenance and performance optimization, aligning with the **digital shadow reference model** for contextual data aggregation and abstraction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides **specialized services** for **friction identification** and **motion accuracy enhancement** of the MABI MAX 100’s first axis. Key services include:
  - **Real-time friction modeling**: Aggregates sensor data (e.g., torque, velocity) to compute **friction coefficients** and **stiction/sliding behavior** via digital shadowing.
  - **Motion accuracy optimization**: Uses **predictive algorithms** (e.g., integrated in the twin’s simulation layer) to correct **joint-level deviations** caused by friction, improving **cyclical positioning accuracy**.
  - **Integrated visualization**: Enables **unified dashboard-based visualization** (via **Asset Administration Shells** or similar frameworks) to display **friction maps**, **motion error trajectories**, and **corrective action recommendations** for operators and maintenance teams.
  - **Data-driven decision support**: Provides **anomaly detection** for friction spikes or wear patterns, triggering **automated alerts** or **adaptive control adjustments**.
  The target users include **robot programmers**, **maintenance engineers**, and **production supervisors**, with capabilities for **offline simulation**, **online correction**, and **historical performance trend analysis**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation leverages the following **tooling and enablers**:
  - **Modeling & Architecture**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for **functional and logical architecture** modeling, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to represent component interactions (e.g., joint actuators, sensors).
    - **UML class diagrams** (as referenced in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**) to define **data structures** for contextual traces (e.g., friction coefficients, motion errors).
  - **Simulation & Analysis**:
    - **MATLAB Simulink** for **dynamic system simulation**, enabling **co-simulation** of the physical twin’s **kinematic and friction models** with real-time data streams.
    - **General-purpose languages (C++/Java)** for **custom algorithm integration** (e.g., friction compensation logic).
  - **Data Acquisition & Protocols**:
    - **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** for **low-latency, bidirectional data streaming** between the physical axis and the Digital Twin, ensuring **real-time synchronization** of sensor readings (e.g., torque, encoder data).
  - **Digital Twin Framework**:
    - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for **standardized data modeling**, enabling **interoperability** with **Industry 4.0 platforms** and **unified visualization dashboards**.
  - **Ontology & Metadata**:
    - **Contextual data aggregation** via **ontology-driven abstraction** (implied in the Digital Shadow Reference Model) to map raw sensor data (e.g., voltage, current) to **semantic friction/failure modes** for predictive analytics.
  The enablers collectively support **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (e.g., engineers, operators) and **multi-tool workflows** (SysML → Simulink → AAS) for end-to-end Digital Twin lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents leverages **multi-layered modeling abstractions** (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) as part of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**. While the documents do not explicitly address **multiple Digital Twin instances** or **decentralized architectures**, they emphasize **hierarchical decomposition** of system models using methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **SysML, ACME, AADL, or MontiArc**. These frameworks inherently support **component-based decomposition** and **modularity**, enabling hierarchical structuring of system representations. However, explicit details on **coordination mechanisms** between twin instances or **scope of responsibilities** are not provided in the context of Digital Twin constellations.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems through **heterogeneous modeling tools and notations**, including **SysML, MathWorks Simulink, and general-purpose programming languages (e.g., C++, Java)**. The documents reference **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, and MontiArc**, which facilitate **component-connector modeling** and **interoperability** between system elements. While **MQTT** is mentioned in passing as a potential data streaming protocol (implied by the context of digital shadows and real-time data aggregation), no explicit **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, IIoT protocols) or **enterprise integration patterns** (e.g., Industry 4.0 frameworks) are detailed. The system’s integration relies on **multi-tool ecosystems** and **stakeholder-specific perspectives**, requiring **data exchange mechanisms** to bridge disparate modeling languages and simulation tools. However, concrete **integration patterns** or **dependencies on external systems** (e.g., ERP, MES) are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **heterogeneous computational models and data components** structured across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies. Key model types include:
  - **Geometric models** (implicitly referenced via architectural descriptions, though not explicitly detailed in the document).
  - **Behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in **SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton**) for dynamic system behavior.
  - **Analytical models** (e.g., integrated with **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools like those referenced in [29], [30]) for performance/physics-based analysis.
  Data sources are derived from **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives**, aggregated via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) [17]**, which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition. Model relationships are established through **cross-tool integration**, where SysML/ADLs serve as the primary structural framework, supplemented by simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) for detailed behavioral analysis. Data management approaches emphasize **abstraction and aggregation** (as per the digital shadow definition in [32]), with models acting as active components to process incoming data traces for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing context).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **physical actuators** primarily through a **permanent magnet synchronous motor** as the primary drive mechanism. Control is managed via a **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** controller, which governs the robot’s operational dynamics. Safety constraints are enforced by a **safety cell**, restricting the maximum working range to **[-90°, 90°]**. No explicit details on remote control interfaces, actuation ranges beyond the safety cell, or communication protocols (e.g., PLC integration) are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes a **dual-encoder architecture** per drive, comprising a **motor encoder** mounted directly on the motor and a **secondary encoder** positioned behind the gearbox. Current sensors are employed to measure motor current, which is converted into applied torque using **known motor constants**. No specifications regarding **resolution, sampling frequencies, data transmission rates, accuracy, or protocols** are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The physical-to-virtual interaction in this Digital Twin system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined by the Digital Shadow Reference Model. Data is transmitted via **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** for real-time streaming of contextual data traces, including sensor readings, operational metrics, and environmental parameters. The data flow is unidirectional, meaning the digital entity does not initiate communication back to the physical system. No explicit details on data types beyond 'contextual data traces,' transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance mechanisms, or integration with MBSE tools (e.g., SysML, AADL) are provided in the document. The aggregation and abstraction of data are purpose-driven but not further specified in terms of technical preprocessing steps or validation protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical network infrastructure for this Digital Twin system is not explicitly detailed beyond the use of **MQTT for data streaming** from the physical to the digital domain. No information is provided regarding network topology (e.g., star, mesh), communication protocols (e.g., OPC UA, RESTful APIs), bandwidth requirements, latency constraints, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols (e.g., TLS, VPN). The document solely references MQTT as the protocol for one-way data transmission, with no mention of bidirectional or control-oriented communication channels.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The development follows methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**, which systematically decompose system complexity. Transition management between phases is facilitated via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition hierarchies. Behavior modeling integrates **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed simulations are executed using tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (C++, Java). Lifecycle governance involves integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and ensuring consistency across **heterogeneous modeling notations and tools**, as emphasized by the need for **interoperability** in MBSE frameworks. The lifecycle transitions from abstract conceptual models to executable digital representations, with governance mechanisms ensuring traceability and alignment with physical system evolution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin evolution** are underpinned by **MBSE methodologies**, where the DT is iteratively developed through structured abstraction layers (requirements → functional → logical → physical). Quality assurance is implicitly embedded via **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** and **behavioral modeling tools (SysML state machines, Simulink)**, ensuring consistency and correctness. Testing and validation are likely conducted via **simulation-based approaches** (e.g., [29], [30]) and **code-level validation** in languages like **C++/Java**. Version control and evolution management are not explicitly detailed in the document, but the reliance on **heterogeneous modeling tools** suggests the use of **model versioning systems** (e.g., **Git for models, DOORS for requirements**) and **model transformation frameworks** (e.g., **ATL, QVT**) to manage DT updates. The document emphasizes **active components** in DTs (unlike digital shadows) to process and push data back to physical systems, implying **real-time synchronization mechanisms** and **continuous evolution** via feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The document does not explicitly describe **decision support capabilities** or **insights generation** for Digital Twins. However, it implies that DTs provide **active components** (e.g., [35]–[37]) to process data and enable **closed-loop interactions** with physical systems. Insights may be derived from **aggregated contextual data traces** (as defined for digital shadows) and **simulation outputs** (e.g., from **Simulink** or other tools). Decision-making processes are likely supported via **analytics engines** (e.g., **Python-based data processing, MATLAB/Simulink for predictive modeling**) and **user interaction methods** (e.g., **dashboard visualization via SysML or custom interfaces**). Confidence measures for recommendations are not specified, but the **MBSE framework** ensures traceability from requirements to behavior, potentially enabling **formal verification** of DT-generated insights.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The digital twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates **safety-critical physical constraints** and **redundant sensor-based mechanisms** to mitigate positional uncertainty and operational risks. The **safety cell** physically restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to a rotational span of [-90°, 90°], ensuring compliance with operational boundaries. To enhance positional accuracy and reduce backlash, the system employs **dual position encoders per drive**: a primary motor encoder mounted directly on the permanent magnet synchronous motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. The differential analysis between these encoders enables real-time detection of **drive train elasticity and friction**, thereby improving torque estimation (τ_M, τ_G, τ_out) and reducing positional uncertainty. While the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl. CNC controller enforces axis-specific constraints (position, speed, acceleration), no explicit cybersecurity protocols (e.g., authentication, encryption) or fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., emergency stop integration) are documented in the provided context. The system’s safety considerations are thus **hardware-constrained and sensor-driven**, relying on physical and kinematic redundancy rather than software-based safeguards.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is specific and aligns with the source, which describes the MABI MAX 100 robot, its first axis, and the focus on friction modeling for motion accuracy. However, some details like 'real-time kinematic and dynamic behavior' are inferred rather than explicitly stated, and the extraction includes some extrapolation beyond the explicit text.

The extraction accurately captures the services described: friction modeling, accuracy optimization, visualization, and decision support. The source mentions 'friction identification', 'improving motion accuracy', and 'integrated visualization in a dashboard', but the extraction adds some detail not explicitly stated, such as 'real-time friction modeling' as a service, though it is implied.

The extraction is highly detailed and specific, with all tools and enablers (SysML, UML, MATLAB Simulink, MQTT, Asset Administration Shells) directly supported by the source text. The extraction is comprehensive and well-grounded in the evidence.

one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.


IV. A PPLICATION OF D IGITAL S HADOW C ONCEPTS IN

P RACTICE
